key_facts: Matter 5 (STK-CV-UF-2026-0001032) targets HOPE Psychotherapy Inc. and Katie Palacios as private defendants in San Joaquin Superior Court — no government claim required because defendants are private actors.

Matter 6 (2:26-cv-00195-TLN-AC) is the federal counterpart filed in USDC E.D. Cal., asserting 42 U.S.C. § 1983 Procedural Due Process against a broader defendant roster: HOPE Psychotherapy Inc., Katie Palacios, Macomber, Bishop, Dodd, Moua, Noguchi, Derrick, and Rojo.

HOPE Psychotherapy Inc. operates a fee-for-service billing model: $85 per group session and $600 per polygraph examination, generating an estimated financial incentive of approximately $9,690 per year to retain participants rather than discharge them.

The blanket retention policy — keeping participants indefinitely regardless of individualized risk or progress — was admitted under oath in the Dwyer proceeding and has been judicially condemned in that decision.
Katie Palacios was personally served on February 24, 2026, by the San Joaquin County Sheriff (POS-010 filed March 3, 2026).
HOPE Psychotherapy Inc., d/b/a HOPE Program, was served on March 26, 2026, via a signed Notice of Acknowledgment and Receipt (NAR) — service complete as to all named defendants in Matter 5.

Sims, Lawrence & Broghammer (SLB) appeared for Palacios in the state case and stipulated to a 30-day extension of the response deadline to April 27, 2026 (state case STK-CV-UF-2026-1032 only; does NOT extend to federal matter 2:26-cv-00004-DJC-JDP).
Matter 5 fee waiver was approved; summons issued; CMC set for October 13, 2026.
Original state response deadline for Palacios was March 26, 2026; extended by written stipulation to April 27, 2026.
Matter 6's Discharge Review Report (Form 1502-DR) is alleged to be facially defective.
Defendants in Matter 6 fabricated a '10-year parole term' ineligibility rule to block discharge — a rule that does not exist in statute or regulation.
Matter 6 is described as the broadest systemic challenge in the litigation cluster, targeting the entire discharge review apparatus used by CDCR and its contracted providers.
HOPE sessions are conducted in camera-equipped rooms, creating potential documentary evidence.

Katie Palacios directly facilitated group sessions after the assigned clinician abruptly resigned; she remarked that Plaintiff was 'doing better' when he spoke less — a statement corroborating the First Amendment retaliation theory in the parallel federal matter (Matter 3).
Palacios jointly revoked Plaintiff's standard 10-minute grace period (early April 2025) alongside Derrick, stating: 'You are clearly abusing the 10-minute as an excuse to show up late.'
Palacios labeled Plaintiff a 'ringleader' whose speech encouraged others to speak — used as negative input in the CTM meeting and the November 2025 Discharge Review.
No qualified immunity is available to HOPE Psychotherapy Inc. or Katie Palacios in their private-defendant capacity in Matter 5.
West v. Atkins supports the state-actor theory applicable to HOPE in Matter 6, where HOPE performs a delegated governmental function ([REDACTED] treatment under parole conditions).

Matter 6 defendants include high-level CDCR officials (Macomber, Bishop, Dodd, Moua, Noguchi, Derrick, Rojo), making it a systemic constitutional challenge rather than an individual-capacity tort suit.
Damages sought in both matters: $250,000–$750,000+ each.
Key advantage in Matter 5 identified in source data: no qualified or sovereign immunity defense available against private defendant HOPE Psychotherapy Inc.

dates.event: Matter 6 (federal) filed — Newanforbi v. HOPE et al., 2:26-cv-00195-TLN-AC, USDC E.D. Cal.
Matter 5 (state) filed — Newanforbi v. Palacios et al., STK-CV-UF-2026-0001032, San Joaquin Superior Court
Matter 5: Katie Palacios personally served by San Joaquin County Sheriff
Matter 5: POS-010 filed with court following Palacios personal service
Matter 5: Original Palacios response deadline (prior to extension)
Matter 5: HOPE Psychotherapy Inc., d/b/a HOPE Program, served — NAR signed and returned executed; service complete as to all named defendants
Matter 5: SLB (Sims, Lawrence & Broghammer) contacted requesting 30-day extension for Palacios state response
Matter 5: Palacios state response deadline extended by stipulation to April 27, 2026 (state case STK-CV-UF-2026-1032 only)

arguments: Matter 5 — Fraud / Misrepresentation: HOPE and Palacios made material misrepresentations regarding the basis and necessity of Plaintiff's continued retention in the [REDACTED] treatment program.
Matter 5 — Negligence: HOPE and Palacios failed to exercise reasonable care in administering treatment and conducting individualized assessments.
Matter 5 — False Imprisonment: Plaintiff's compelled participation in the HOPE program, based on fabricated or legally baseless retention criteria, constitutes unlawful restraint of liberty.

Matter 5 — Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress (IIED): HOPE and Palacios engaged in extreme and outrageous conduct — including the 'ringleader' characterization, retaliatory session facilitation, and collaboration with parole agents to suppress speech — causing severe emotional distress.

Matter 5 — Economic Interference: HOPE's and Palacios's conduct, including denial of scheduling accommodations and retaliatory input into the discharge review, interfered with Plaintiff's employment relationships and earning capacity.

Matter 6 — Procedural Due Process (14th Amendment): Systemic challenge to the discharge review framework; defendants denied Plaintiff a constitutionally adequate process before continued detention in the treatment program.

Matter 6 — Fabricated ineligibility rule: Defendants invented a '10-year parole term' disqualification with no basis in statute or regulation, depriving Plaintiff of a discharge determination on the merits.

Matter 6 — Delegated state-actor liability (West v. Atkins): HOPE Psychotherapy Inc. performs a function traditionally reserved to the government ([REDACTED] treatment mandated by parole conditions), rendering it a state actor subject to § 1983 liability.

Matter 6 — Facial defect in Form 1502-DR: The Discharge Review Report used to deny discharge contains scoring errors and retaliatory inputs that render it constitutionally insufficient as a basis for continued restraint.

Both matters — Dwyer judicial condemnation: HOPE's blanket retention policy has already been found unlawful in Dwyer; continued enforcement of the same policy exposes defendants to liability for knowing violation of established rights.

Both matters — Fee-for-service financial conflict of interest: The $85/group and $600/polygraph billing structure creates a $9,690/year per-participant incentive that corrupts the clinical judgment required for individualized discharge assessments.

Matter 5 — No immunity defense: As a private entity and individual contractor, HOPE Psychotherapy Inc. and Palacios cannot invoke qualified immunity or sovereign immunity, removing the primary defense available to government defendants in parallel matters.
evidence_cited: Dwyer order — judicial condemnation of HOPE's blanket retention policy, admitted under oath
Alameda Contract — billing rate documentation establishing $85/group session and $600/polygraph fee structure
Form 1502-DR — November 2025 Discharge Review Report; alleged to be facially defective and incorporating retaliatory characterizations from Palacios and Derrick
CTM (Classification and Treatment Meeting) records — Palacios's 'ringleader' characterization and Derrick's negative oral report on Plaintiff's speech used as adverse inputs
HOPE billing records — documents the fee-for-service financial incentive structure
HOPE Group Session Camera Recordings — sessions conducted in camera-equipped rooms at Stockton facility
BCP Fiscal Detail — referenced in Matter 6 to document HOPE's government-funded financial arrangements
NAR (Notice of Acknowledgment and Receipt) — executed by HOPE Psychotherapy Inc., d/b/a HOPE Program, confirming service on March 26, 2026
POS-010 — proof of personal service on Katie Palacios filed March 3, 2026
SLB Stipulation (state case only) — written record of April 27, 2026 extension tethered exclusively to STK-CV-UF-2026-1032
Clinician 'Ms. Jackie' contemporaneous observation — independently noted behavioral change in Plaintiff when Palacios remarked he was 'doing better' when speaking less
procedural_significance:
Matter 5 (STK-CV-UF-2026-0001032, San Joaquin Superior Court) and Matter 6 (2:26-cv-00195-TLN-AC, USDC E.D. Cal.) form a coordinated two-track attack on HOPE Psychotherapy Inc. and Katie Palacios, mirroring the state/federal parallel structure used throughout this litigation portfolio.

Strategically, Matter 5 is the liability-maximizing track: by suing HOPE and Palacios as private defendants in state court, Plaintiff eliminates the qualified immunity and sovereign immunity defenses that shield government actors in every other cluster. The tort claims (fraud, negligence, false imprisonment, IIED, economic interference) allow full compensatory and potentially punitive damages without the constitutional pleading burdens of § 1983. Service is already complete as to both defendants, the response deadline is locked at April 27, 2026 (Palacios) following the SLB stipulation, and the fee waiver is approved — the case is procedurally clean and ready to advance.

Matter 6 is the systemic-reform track: the § 1983 Procedural Due Process claim targets the entire discharge review apparatus at the institutional level, naming not just HOPE and Palacios but also CDCR Secretary Macomber and a slate of administrators (Bishop, Dodd, Moua, Noguchi, Derrick, Rojo). A favorable ruling in Matter 6 could establish that the discharge review framework is unconstitutional as applied, generating precedent that benefits the entire class of affected parolees and providing injunctive relief Plaintiff cannot obtain in state tort litigation.

The two matters reinforce each other evidentially: the Dwyer order, Alameda Contract billing rates, Form 1502-DR, and CTM records are common to both and build a coherent narrative of financially motivated, procedurally defective, and constitutionally infirm retention practices. The fee-for-service billing structure ($9,690/year incentive) is simultaneously a fraud theory in Matter 5 and a due process structural-bias argument in Matter 6. A judgment or adverse finding against HOPE on the Dwyer/blanket-retention theory in Matter 5 would have collateral estoppel and reputational implications for HOPE's defense in Matter 6. Conversely, any declaratory ruling in the federal case on the constitutionality of the discharge framework would strengthen the fraud and false imprisonment predicate in the state case by establishing that the retention was legally unjustified.
